title: HOW BAD IS OCEAN GARBAGE, REALLY?
content_html:
Assessing the threat of marine debris

Chelsea Rochman, an ecologist at the University of California, Davis, has been trying to answer a dismal question: Is everything terrible, or are things just very, very bad?

Rochman is a member of the National Center for Ecological Analysis and Synthesis’s marine-debris working group, a collection of scientists who study, among other things, the growing problem of marine debris, also known as ocean trash. Plenty of studies have sounded alarm bells about the state of marine debris; in a recent paper published in the journal Ecology, Rochman and her colleagues set out to determine how many of those perceived risks are real.

Often, Rochman says, scientists will end a paper by speculating about the broader impacts of what they’ve found. For example, a study could show that certain seabirds eat plastic bags, and go on to warn that whole bird populations are at risk of dying out. ‘But the truth was that nobody had yet tested those perceived threats,’ Rochman says. ‘There wasn’t a lot of information. ’

Rochman and her colleagues examined more than a hundred papers on the impacts of marine debris that were published through 2013. Within each paper, they asked what threats scientists had studied – 366 perceived threats in all – and what they’d actually found.

In 83 percent of cases, the perceived dangers of ocean trash were proven true. In the remaining cases, the working group found the studies had weaknesses in design and content which affected the validity of their conclusions – they lacked a control group, for example, or used faulty statistics.

Strikingly, Rochman says, only one well-designed study failed to find the effect it was looking for, an investigation of mussels ingesting microscopic bits. The plastic moved from the mussels’ stomachs to their bloodstreams, scientists found, and stayed there for weeks – but didn’t seem to stress out the shellfish.

While mussels may be fine eating trash, though, the analysis also gave a clearer picture of the many ways that ocean debris is bothersome.

Within the studies they looked at, most of the proven threats came from plastic debris, rather than other materials like metal or wood. Most of the dangers also involved large pieces of debris – animals getting entangled in trash, for example, or eating it and severely injuring themselves.

But a lot of ocean debris is ‘microplastic’, or pieces smaller than five millimeters. These may be ingredients used in cosmetics and toiletries, fibers shed by synthetic clothing in the wash, or eroded remnants of larger debris. Compared to the number of studies investigating large-scale debris, Rochman’s group found little research on the effects of these tiny bits. ‘There are a lot of open questions still for microplastic,’ Rochman says, though she notes that more papers on the subject have been published since 2013, the cutoff point for the group’s analysis.

There are also, she adds, a lot of open questions about the ways that ocean debris can lead to sea-creature death. Many studies have looked at how plastic affects an individual animal, or that animal’s tissues or cells, rather than whole populations. And in the lab, scientists often use higher concentrations of plastic than what’s really in the ocean. of that tells us how many birds or fish or sea turtles could die from plastic pollution – or how deaths in one species could affect that animal’s predators, or the rest of the ecosystem.

‘We need to be asking more ecologically relevant questions,’ Rochman says. Usually, scientists don’t know exactly how disasters such as a tanker accidentally spilling its whole cargo of oil and polluting huge areas of the ocean will affect the environment until after they’ve happened. ‘We don’t ask the right questions early enough,’ she says. But if ecologists can understand how the slow-moving effect of ocean trash is damaging ecosystems, they might be able to prevent things from getting worse.

Asking the right questions can help policy makers, and the public, figure out where to focus their attention. The problems that look or sound most dramatic may not be the best places to start. For example, the name of the ‘Great Pacific Garbage Patch’ – a collection of marine debris in the northern Pacific Ocean – might conjure up a vast, floating trash island. In reality though, much of the debris is tiny or below the surface; a person could sail through the area without seeing any trash at all. A Dutch group called ‘The Ocean Cleanup’ is currently working on plans to put mechanical devices in the Pacific Garbage Patch and similar areas to suck up plastic. But a recent paper used simulations to show that strategically positioning the cleanup devices closer to shore would more effectively reduce pollution over the long term.

‘I think clearing up some of these misperceptions is really important,’ Rochman says. Among scientists as well as in the media, she says, ‘A lot of the images about strandings and entanglement and all of that cause the perception that plastic debris is killing everything in the ocean. ’ Interrogating the existing scientific literature can help ecologists figure out which problems really need addressing, and which ones they’d be better off – like the mussels – absorbing and ignoring.

questions.groupHtml:
Questions 27–33

Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 27–33 on your answer sheet, write
TRUE  if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE  if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN  if there is no information on this
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “Rochman and her colleagues” / “the first people” / “research / marine debris”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 2: “Plenty of studies have sounded alarm bells about the state of marine debris; in a recent paper published in the journal Ecology, Rochman and her colleagues set out to determine...”
Bước 3: Phân tích: “Plenty of studies” (Nhiều nghiên cứu trước đó) đã gióng lên hồi chuông cảnh báo, đối lập hoàn toàn với câu hỏi khẳng định họ là “the first people” (những người đầu tiên).
=> Đáp án: FALSE

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.question_text: Rochman and her colleagues were the first people to research the problem of marine debris.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “creatures most in danger” / “ocean trash” / “certain seabirds”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 3: “For example, a study could show that certain seabirds eat plastic bags, and go on to warn that whole bird populations are at risk of dying out.”

Bước 3: Phân tích: Bài đọc có nhắc đến việc chim biển ăn túi nilon, nhưng không có bất kỳ thông tin nào so sánh hoặc khẳng định chúng là loài gặp nguy hiểm nhất (most in danger) so với các sinh vật khác.

=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN
questions.question_text: The creatures most in danger from ocean trash are certain seabirds.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “studies Rochman reviewed” / “already proved” / “populations of some birds / soon become extinct”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 3: “...go on to warn that whole bird populations are at risk of dying out. ‘But the truth was that nobody had yet tested those perceived threats,’ Rochman says.”

Bước 3: Phân tích: “already proved” (đã chứng minh) đối lập hoàn toàn với “nobody had yet tested” (chưa một ai kiểm chứng/thử nghiệm).

=> Đáp án: FALSE
questions.question_text: The studies Rochman has reviewed have already proved that populations of some birds will soon become extinct.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “analysed papers” / “different kinds of danger” / “ocean trash”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 4: “Rochman and her colleagues examined more than a hundred papers on the impacts of marine debris... Within each paper, they asked what threats scientists had studied – 366 perceived threats in all...”

Bước 3: Phân tích: “examined more than a hundred papers” = analysed papers; “366 perceived threats” = different kinds of danger. Thông tin trong bài hoàn toàn khớp với câu hỏi.

=> Đáp án: TRUE
questions.question_text: Rochman analysed papers on the different kinds of danger caused by ocean trash.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: 
“Most of the research” / “analysed” / “badly designed”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: 
Paragraph 5: “In 83 percent of cases, the perceived dangers of ocean trash were proven true. In the remaining cases, the working group found the studies had weaknesses in design and content...”

Bước 3: Phân tích: 
Bài đọc nêu rõ 83% nghiên cứu là chính xác, chỉ có số ít các trường hợp còn lại (remaining cases = 17%) là có lỗi thiết kế (weaknesses in design),  đối lập hoàn toàn với từ “Most” (Phần lớn) trong câu hỏi.

=> Đáp án: FALSE
questions.question_text: Most of the research analysed by Rochman and her colleagues was badly designed.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “One study” / “expecting to find” / “mussels were harmed / eating plastic”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 6: “Strikingly, Rochman says, only one well-designed study failed to find the effect it was looking for, an investigation of mussels ingesting microscopic bits... – but didn’t seem to stress out the shellfish.”

Bước 3: Phân tích: “failed to find the effect it was looking for” (thất bại trong việc tìm ra tác động mà nó tìm kiếm) chứng tỏ nghiên cứu này ban đầu kỳ vọng/mong đợi (expecting to find) sẽ thấy tác hại của nhựa lên loài vẹm nhưng thực tế lại không thấy (didn’t seem to stress out).

=> Đáp án: TRUE
questions.question_text: One study examined by Rochman was expecting to find that mussels were harmed by eating plastic.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “mussels choose to eat plastic” / “in preference to” / “natural diet”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 6 & 7: “an investigation of mussels ingesting microscopic bits... While mussels may be fine eating trash...”

Bước 3: Phân tích: Bài đọc chỉ đề cập đến việc loài vẹm vô tình ăn phải hạt nhựa (ingesting microscopic bits), hoàn toàn không có thông tin nào nói về việc chúng chủ động lựa chọn (choose/in preference to) ăn nhựa thay cho thức ăn tự nhiên.

=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN
questions.question_text: Some mussels choose to eat plastic in preference to their natural diet.

questions.groupHtml:
Questions 34–39

Complete the notes below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “biggest threats” / “bits of debris” / “harmful to animals”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 8: “Most of the dangers also involved large pieces of debris – animals getting entangled in trash, for example, or eating it and severely injuring themselves.”

Bước 3: Phân tích: Từ cần điền là một tính từ bổ nghĩa cho “bits of debris” dựa trên ngữ cảnh gây hại trực tiếp cho động vật (injuring themselves). Trong đoạn 8 chỉ ra thủ phạm là “large pieces”.

=> Đáp án: large

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**Studies of marine debris found the biggest threats were**
• plastic (not metal or wood)
• bits of debris that were [34] (harmful to animals)
There was little research into [35] e.g. from synthetic fibres.
**Drawbacks of the studies examined**
• most of them focused on individual animals, not entire [36]
• the [37] of plastic used in the lab did not always reflect those in the ocean
•   there was insufficient information on
–   numbers of animals which could be affected
–   the impact of a reduction in numbers on the [38] of that species
–   the impact on the ecosystem
Rochman says more information is needed on the possible impact of future [39] (e.g. involving oil).

questions.questionHtml:
Findings related to marine debris

Studies of marine debris found the biggest threats were

  • plastic (not metal or wood)
  • bits of debris that were 34_______ (harmful to animals)

There was little research into 35_______ e.g. from synthetic fibres.

Drawbacks of the studies examined

  • most of them focused on individual animals, not entire 36_______
  • the 37_______ of plastic used in the lab did not always reflect those in the ocean
  • there was insufficient information on

– numbers of animals which could be affected

– the impact of a reduction in numbers on the 38_______ of that species

– the impact on the ecosystem

Rochman says more information is needed on the possible impact of future 39_______ (e.g. involving oil).
questions.question_text: Studies found the biggest threats were plastic — bits of debris that were 34. ___________ (harmful to animals)

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “little research into” / “synthetic fibres”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 9: “These may be ingredients used in cosmetics and toiletries, fibers shed by synthetic clothing in the wash... Rochman’s group found little research on the effects of these tiny bits. ‘There are a lot of open questions still for microplastic,’ Rochman says...”

Bước 3: Phân tích: Từ “little research” trùng khớp hoàn toàn trong bài. “These tiny bits” và “open questions” ở đây dùng để chỉ “microplastic” đã được định nghĩa ở đầu đoạn 9.

=> Đáp án: microplastic
questions.question_text: There was little research into 35. ___________ e.g. from synthetic fibres.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “focused on individual animals” / “not entire”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 10: “Many studies have looked at how plastic affects an individual animal, or that animal’s tissues or cells, rather than whole populations.”

Bước 3: Phân tích: Cấu trúc đối lập “rather than whole populations” (thay vì toàn bộ quần thể) đồng nghĩa với cụm “not entire [noun]” trong câu hỏi. Do đó từ cần điền là danh từ số nhiều.

=> Đáp án: populations
questions.question_text: most of them focused on individual animals, not entire 36. ___________

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “plastic used in the lab” / “did not always reflect” / “ocean”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 10: “And in the lab, scientists often use higher concentrations of plastic than what’s really in the ocean.”

Bước 3: Phân tích: Việc dùng “higher... than what’s really in the ocean” đồng nghĩa với việc nó không phản ánh đúng thực tế (did not reflect). Cái được mang ra so sánh ở đây chính là lượng/nồng độ nhựa.

=> Đáp án: concentrations
questions.question_text: the 37. ___________ of plastic used in the lab did not always reflect those in the ocean

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “insufficient information” / “reduction in numbers” / “impact on... of that species”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 10: “None of that tells us how many birds or fish or sea turtles could die from plastic pollution – or how deaths in one species could affect that animal’s predators...”

Bước 3: Phân tích: Cụm “None of that tells us” = insufficient information; “how many... could die” = reduction in numbers. Tác động trực tiếp tiếp theo được nhắc tới là đối với các loài săn mồi (predators).

=> Đáp án: predators
questions.question_text: the impact of a reduction in numbers on the 38. ___________ of that species

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “more information is needed” / “possible impact of future” / “involving oil”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Paragraph 11: “Usually, scientists don’t know exactly how disasters such as a tanker accidentally spilling its whole cargo of oil and polluting huge areas of the ocean will affect the environment until after they’ve happened.”  

Bước 3: Phân tích: Việc “don't know until after they've happened” chính là lý do cần “more information”. Ví dụ tràn dầu được mô tả trong bài chính là một dạng tai họa/thảm họa (disasters).

=> Đáp án: disasters
questions.question_text: Rochman says more information is needed on the possible impact of future 39. ___________ (e.g. involving oil).

options: Assessing the threat of marine debris
Marine debris: who is to blame?
A new solution to the problem of marine debris
Marine debris: the need for international action
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: “best title” / “this passage”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn: Toàn bộ bài đọc (từ tiêu đề gốc đến nội dung tổng quát của các nghiên cứu mà Rochman tổng hợp lại).

Bước 3: Phân tích: Bài đọc tập trung mổ xẻ xem các mối đe dọa từ rác thải đại dương cái nào là thật, cái nào là phóng đại dựa trên số liệu khoa học → Đây chính là hành động “đánh giá mối đe dọa” (Assessing the threat). Các lựa chọn B, C, D đều sai vì bài không quy trách nhiệm cho ai (B), không tập trung vào giải pháp mới (C - chỉ nhắc qua dự án Ocean Cleanup ở cuối như một ví dụ), và không kêu gọi hợp tác quốc tế (D).

=> Đáp án: A

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.question_text: What would be the best title for this passage?
